artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

I just made some tea and finally sat down to catch you up a bit. It’s been a weird mix of good and hard lately.

I had another session with Dr. Karen Lewis at the University of Calgary Wellness Centre this afternoon. We ended up talking again about Nana Maggie’s last six months at Providence Manor in Kingston and how I still replay not being there more in late 2022 when her vascular dementia got worse and she went on that palliative care plan. I told her I still picture the halls there and feel this wave of guilt. She keeps reminding me I was in school and doing the best I could, but it’s hard to shake.

We also talked about how I still get those panic-y feelings around hospitals and care homes. It’s better than it was right after Nana died, but even walking into the nursing sim lab here can set me off some days. Dr. Lewis thinks the way I’ve been journaling and walking the Bow River pathways helps, but she also suggested that spending regular time with long-term care residents in a more supported way might slowly untangle some of the anxiety.

I told her about how much those volunteers meant to Nana at Providence Manor – the music afternoons, people wheeling her to the garden, just sitting and listening. I keep thinking it would be good to be that person for someone else. Part of me also wants to see what day-to-day life is actually like in a continuing care place, not just the scary bits from when someone is actively dying.

Anyway, I’m okay, just tired. I’m going to read a chapter of my historical novel and then crash. Give Dad and Sophie a hug for me.

Love,
Emily

Hey Ryan,

I’m curled up on the couch at Willow Park trying to decompress from today, so of course I’m procrastinating by writing you a novel instead of doing pharm readings.

I can’t stop thinking about that geriatric sim in the UCalgary nursing lab back in October 2024 – the one on the “geriatric unit” setup where I completely froze. I still hear the monitor beeping and remember my chest tightening, and then Prof. Helen McKay stepping in, pulling me out of the room, and quietly suggesting we cut my clinical hours from 16 a week to 8 until I felt steadier. I know it was “just” a simulation, but it felt like failing in front of everyone.

Part of why I keep talking about volunteering at Carewest is that I need some kind of real-world, lower-pressure contact with older adults so that incident doesn’t end up defining my future in nursing. I want to be around seniors in a way that isn’t all crisis and performance – more walking to activities, chatting over cards, maybe playing guitar for a small group.

On a totally different note, I feel like my whole life is either nursing or Starbucks right now. When I’m not on shift at Macleod Trail or in class, I’m binging true crime shows and horror movies on Netflix or scrolling TikTok instead of doing anything remotely productive. I know that’s not exactly a “healthy coping strategy,” but my brain just checks out.

Anyway, are we still on for a Bow River walk this weekend if the weather doesn’t suck? I could use a long walk and a coffee that I didn’t have to make myself.

– Em

Hey Jess,

Long overdue update from Calgary while I half-watch some terrible true crime doc and pretend I’m not behind on patho.

Living in the basement suite on Willow Park Drive SE since May 2024 has been…a lot lonelier than I expected. The novelty of “I have my own place in Calgary!” wore off and now it’s mostly me, my textbooks, and way too much scrolling. Things are still pretty tense with Mom and Dad about me not staying in Kingston to help more with Sophie when her depression was really bad last year. I get why they were scared, but I also needed to actually live my own life.

I’ve been thinking a lot about finding some kind of community that isn’t just Starbucks or nursing classmates. I keep coming back to long-term care because of Nana Maggie’s time at Providence Manor and later at Providence Care Hospital. Remember when we used to visit her on that secured unit and I’d bring my guitar for the sing-alongs? Part of me misses that feeling of doing something that clearly mattered to someone.

At the same time, I’m trying to be strategic. Volunteering in rehab or continuing care would look pretty good when I’m trying to get more competitive placements later, and it’d give me a chance to figure out if I actually want to work in that environment or if it would burn me out.

When I’m not at school or work I mostly just lie in bed, scroll Reddit and TikTok for hours, and then feel gross about wasting the day. It’s like I swing between over-functioning nurse mode and complete blob.

Anyway, how’s Kingston? Send me a picture of the lake if you walk by it – I miss it more than I thought I would.

Love,
Em

metadata.subject: Life from the Willow Park cave

artifacts.content:
Late-night planning brain dump – saved in Notes

I keep circling back to why I’m so obsessed with getting experience in rehab/continuing care.

1) Uncle Patrick at Kingston General Hospital still haunts me. Summer 2021 on the post-op rehab unit, that medication error that left him confused and agitated for days, nurses rushing past, no one really sitting with him or explaining what was happening. Our whole family felt like we were in the way. If I ever end up working for Alberta Health Services, I want to know from the inside what good advocacy looks like so I don’t repeat that.

2) I’m also low-key worried about my own “record.” Starbucks HR still has that three-week medical leave from Feb–Mar 2024 for the stress-related insomnia. Dr. Ahmed at the South Calgary Primary Care Network clinic put me on zopiclone so I could sleep, and my manager had to file the leave. I know it’s not some huge scandal, but I hate that the only formal thing attached to my name at work is a mental health blip.

3) It would honestly help my resume a lot to show that I can handle a consistent schedule in a care environment, not just a coffee shop. If I can stick with regular shifts, it’ll prove to me (and future AHS managers) that I’m reliable even when my anxiety flares.

Side note: I really need to stop imagining the perfect volunteer role. I keep thinking, “Anywhere that doesn’t require a massive time commitment or tons of extra training would be ideal,” because school and Starbucks already eat my life.

Goal: find something that stretches me but doesn’t tip me back into insomnia land.

metadata.title: Why I care so much about rehab/continuing care

artifacts.content:
Hi Sophie,

Just finished a Bow River walk (my feet hate me) and thought I’d write before I dive into more readings.

I’ve been on a bit of a “processing Nana” kick again. Do you remember those three weeks in January 2022 when we basically lived at Providence Manor in Kingston with her while she was on palliative care? Sleeping on those awful fold-out chairs, helping Mom talk to the hospice social worker, making sure her pain meds were actually given on time. That whole stretch is burned into my brain.

I’ve been journaling a lot about it, especially after that geriatric sim meltdown in October 2024 at the UCalgary nursing simulation lab. Freezing in front of Prof. McKay and everyone, then having my clinical hours cut from 16 to 8 per week, shook me way more than I admitted at the time. I keep wondering if it’s all tied together – Nana’s decline, Providence Care and Providence Manor, Uncle Patrick’s mess at Kingston General, and now my panic episodes during anything hospital-ish.

One thing that helps is walking ridiculous distances along the Bow River pathways when my anxiety spikes – I did almost 10 km today after a rough lab. It’s the only time my brain seems to quiet down. That and our little book club here; in March we met every Sunday evening in the Cascade Hall lounge to talk about “Being Mortal” and how much it reminded me of Nana’s DNR decision at Providence Care in 2022.

Also, totally random, but I’ve realized I like going out for drinks and late nights with friends more than I want to admit – which means I sleep in and avoid early mornings whenever I can. Not exactly the image of the perfect future nurse.

Miss you. How’s Regi? Text me a photo of your latest art project.

Love,
Em

Event: Trauma-informed yoga + Bow River walk

Date: Wednesday, 2024-05-15
Time: 5:30 p.m. – 8:30 p.m.
Location: Start at Yoga Santosha (4th Street SW), then Bow River pathways
Attendees: Emily

Notes:

5:30–6:45 p.m. – Trauma-informed yoga class at Yoga Santosha on 4th Street SW. Keep going every Wednesday evening like I promised myself back in March 2024. This has been one of the only things that really helped with the panic attacks that started after Grandma Maggie O’Leary’s decline and passing at the long-term care home in Kingston in January 2023.

After class, walk from Mission down to the Bow River pathways and do at least 6–8 km. Long walks are still my best reset after tough days in the University of Calgary nursing simulation lab or after arguments with Ryan about his drinking (especially those July–August 2024 fights). Bring headphones and queue up the mental health podcasts.

Reminder: try not to bail on this just because I’m tired or tempted to stay home and binge creepy mystery podcasts and TikTok. I already spend way too many evenings just zoning out with true-crime shows instead of actually doing anything that makes me feel grounded.

To bring:
- Water bottle
- Small snack
- Transit card
- Journal (in case I want to jot down thoughts after walking)

Optional: if I’m not wiped, stop at Starbucks on Macleod Trail SE on the way home to say hi to whoever’s on bar and grab a tea (no caffeine after 7 p.m., remember the insomnia flare in Feb–Mar 2024).

Ryan: You alive under that pile of nursing notes?

Emily: Barely. I’m at my place on Willow Park with a cold coffee and a mountain of readings.

Ryan: You still thinking of picking up more shifts at Starbucks or are you actually going to rest this term?

Emily: Weirdly, I’m leaning toward fewer Macleod Trail shifts and more structured stuff that actually lines up with nursing.

Ryan: Like what?

Emily: I’ve been looking at doing regular mornings in a continuing care/rehab setting. After what happened with Uncle Patrick at Kingston General’s post-op rehab unit in summer 2021 – that med error that left him confused and agitated for days and nobody really sitting with him – I keep thinking I want to learn how GOOD systems work.

Emily: Plus, Uncle P, Nana’s time at Providence Manor and Providence Care, and then my own mess – the geriatric sim where I froze in Oct 2024 and Prof. McKay cut my clinical hours from 16 down to 8/week – it all kind of pushes me toward facing this stuff instead of running away from it.

Ryan: That’s a lot.

Emily: Yeah. Also, practical side: it would look amazing when I’m trying to get competitive placements. I’m not going to lie, I’m definitely thinking about what makes me stand out.

Ryan: Fair.

Emily: I just have to be careful with time. My ideal would be something that doesn’t involve tons of direct resident interaction every second or endless extra training – like a mix of helping with mobility groups and maybe some behind-the-scenes stuff – so I can sometimes sneak in readings if it’s quiet and not totally tank my GPA.

Ryan: Classic you, trying to turn volunteering into a study hall lol.

Emily: Guilty. But also trying really hard not to burn out again like last Feb–Mar with the insomnia and zopiclone.

Ryan: Okay, let’s sit down this weekend and map your week so you don’t implode.

Emily: Deal.

metadata.title: Texts with Ryan – volunteering vs. sanity

artifacts.content:
From: emily.thompson@ucalgary.ca
To: mom.laura.thompson@example.com
Cc: sophie.thompson@example.com, david.thompson@example.com
Date: 2026-02-05
Subject: Church music, Nana, Sophie’s project, and money stuff

Hi Mom,

Long catch‑up while I drink my oatmeal latte before class.

Church has been really grounding. Since March 2024, Father Michael at St. Brigid’s Catholic Church in Willow Park, Calgary has had me lead worship with my acoustic guitar about twice a month. He specifically asked me to start including some of the older hymns Nana Maggie (Margaret O’Leary) loved when she was in the long-term care unit at Providence Care Hospital in Kingston. The first time I played one of those hymns I almost cried thinking about her palliative care days back in late 2022.

Speaking of Nana, in February 2024 I spent about 10 hours at the apartment here at 4823 Willow Park Drive SE putting together that scrapbook from her last months in the Kingston long-term care facility. I finally finished it and mailed Sophie a digital copy. Soph, I’m glad you and Ms. Harris at Regiopolis-Notre Dame Catholic High School were okay with using short excerpts from Nana’s 2022 palliative care journal for your Grade 12 English presentation this January 2025, but please keep everything anonymous and private.

On the money side: I updated my budget spreadsheet (started back in May 2024 when I was still in residence at UCalgary). I logged my latest Starbucks, Macleod Trail SE paycheque and the OSAP loan disbursement, and I can still send the $150 a month to help with the overdue hydro bill and rent increase on the apartment in Kingston. It’s tight, but doable.

Love you all and miss you.

Emily

metadata.subject: Church music, Nana, Sophie’s project, and money stuff

artifacts.content:
From: emily.thompson@ucalgary.ca
To: ryan.chen@example.com
Date: 2026-02-08
Subject: Volunteering plans + Starbucks + brain stuff

Hey Ry,

I was mapping out the next year and thought I’d loop you in so we can still squeeze in Bow River walks.

Starbucks first: since October 2023 when Kelly (my manager at the Starbucks on Macleod Trail SE) started the store-sponsored open mic nights, I’ve been hosting most Thursday evenings from 7–9 p.m. She even keeps me on the clock for the first 1 hour so I can help run it and then play a couple of folk covers. I also sometimes try out the acoustic songs I’ve been writing with Dr. Patel at the University of Calgary Wellness Centre—remember, since January 2024 we’ve been turning my second-year nursing burnout journal entries into songs, and I’ve shared a few at small church gatherings.

The downside: after that customer complaint in October 2025 about me casually mixing up their medication instructions while chatting about my clinical placement, Kelly cut my Thursday morning shifts at the Macleod Trail Starbucks. The silver lining is I can now commit a solid 4 hours on Thursday mornings to volunteering.

I told Carewest I’d love to help with anxiety/depression support groups because I’ve been in weekly CBT with Dr. Patel at the UCalgary Wellness Centre since October 2023 for panic attacks around clinical placements. I also mentioned I’m into fall-prevention and mobility programs after helping Mom care for Grandma Maggie following her hip fracture at Kingston General Hospital in March 2021—seeing how much physio and supervision she needed really stuck with me.

Talk later?

Em

From: emily.thompson@ucalgary.ca
To: self-journal@notebook.app
Date: 2026-02-10
Subject: Volunteering capacity + family health + mental health tools

Entry – save to personal file.

Fall 2025 was a reality check. With Dad’s mild stroke in July 2025 at Kingston General Hospital, I was spending about 10 hours a week on Zoom from Calgary going over his physio homework and medication reminders. That’s why I limited myself to one 3-hour volunteer shift most weeks that semester – anything more felt unsafe.

Looking ahead: for Winter 2026, I told the Carewest coordinator I can usually do 3–4 hour shifts on Tuesday and Thursday mornings. My Nursing 285 clinical rotation at Foothills Medical Centre runs 2:00–8:00 p.m. those days, and I need buffer time to bus from my place near the University of Calgary campus. During Reading Week in February 2026, I’m planning two 4-hour shifts because I’m staying in Calgary instead of going home to Kingston. My psychiatrist, Dr. Patel at the University of Calgary Student Wellness Centre, wants me to keep a structured daytime schedule while we adjust my SSRI dosage so my anxiety doesn’t spike.

I’m also reflecting on how long this anxiety has been around. My family doctor at the Kingston family medicine clinic first linked it to the insomnia and panic episodes that started during KCVI exam season in 2021 and suggested the paid version of Headspace; I signed up in October 2023 for about $90 for the year and it’s helped. Meanwhile, I’m still journaling almost every night here at 4823 Willow Park Drive SE about shifts at the Starbucks on Macleod Trail SE. Since Spring 2023 I’ve noticed more regulars with dementia-like symptoms, including one man in May 2024 who got so confused about whether he’d already paid. Those moments, plus informal knitting visits with Mrs. Donovan (early-stage Alzheimer’s) for 2 hours every Thursday at Lakeside Manor Retirement Residence over winter break 2023–2024, keep pulling me toward seniors’ mental health and end-of-life care.

Summer 2026 might finally be lighter: Grandma Maggie O’Leary’s estate settled in May 2026 in Kingston, so I won’t be stuck with $300 monthly credit card payments for flights back and forth during her long-term care. That means fewer 6-hour Starbucks shifts and room for 3–4 hour volunteer shifts three times a week.

—Emily

metadata.subject: Volunteering capacity + family health + mental health tools

artifacts.content:
From: emily.thompson@ucalgary.ca
To: maya.rodriguez@ucalgary.ca
Date: 2024-03-18
Subject: August plans + quick question about shift swaps

Hey Maya,

I was looking at my calendar after lab today and wanted to give you a heads up about my August plans so we can coordinate Starbucks shifts.

Like usual, I’m planning to be back in Kingston for about 10 days in August 2023 was Aug 5–15, and I’m aiming for something similar this year. I’ll be staying at my parents’ place on Earl Street in Kingston, Ontario with my younger sister, Sophie Thompson. It’s become a bit of a tradition for me and Sophie to have that chunk of time together before her school year starts.

While I’m there, I’m hoping to go back to the same long-term care home my mom, Laura Thompson, talks about all the time from her family medicine clinic. Her clinic refers a lot of elderly patients there, and last summer I brought my guitar and played folk songs during the afternoon recreation hour on the dementia unit. It was honestly one of the highlights of my year and made me even more sure about going into geriatric and rehab nursing.

If I submit a time-off request for those ~10 days, do you think it’ll be easier on the schedule if I front-load more Macleod Trail SE shifts earlier in August, or spread them out in July instead? I don’t want to leave everyone scrambling.

Thanks for any advice (and for possibly grabbing one of my Friday mornings if needed!).

Emily
4823 Willow Park Drive SE
Calgary, AB T2J 1P7

metadata.subject: August plans + quick question about shift swaps

artifacts.content:
From: emily.thompson@ucalgary.ca
To: ryan.chen@ucalgary.ca
Date: 2024-11-07
Subject: Midterm fallout + what Dr. Patel suggested

Hey Ryan,

I wanted to explain why I’ve been a bit off the grid since the last patho midterm. I ended up having two full-on panic attacks during midterms in October, one right after our pharm exam and another the week after. It scared me enough that I booked in with my family doctor at University of Calgary Student Wellness Services (Dr. Patel).

Dr. Patel was actually really kind and practical. She suggested I try working through Dr. Kristin Neff’s self-compassion workbook instead of just white-knuckling my way through the semester. So through October 2024 and now into November 2024, I’ve been doing 20–30 minutes of journaling most nights. I’m using the workbook exercises to reflect on clinical simulations, the Starbucks shifts that leave me wired, and just my general anxiety about grades and placements.

It’s weirdly helpful to write out the self-compassion stuff instead of just beating myself up for not being “tough enough.” I’m noticing the panic feelings earlier now, especially on those days when I’m running from a lab straight to Macleod Trail SE for a rush-hour shift.

I know we had planned more Bow River walks and movie nights, but I’ve needed some quiet evenings at 4823 Willow Park Drive SE to journal and decompress. I’d still really like to see you this weekend if you’re up for something low-key—maybe cooking at your place and then I can bring my notebook and finish my Neff workbook reflection while you’re tinkering with your mech design project?

Love,
Emily

metadata.subject: Midterm fallout + what Dr. Patel suggested

artifacts.content:
Title: Personal statement + volunteer interests brainstorm
Date: 2024-12-02

Drafting ideas for my Carewest / AHS volunteer and future practicum applications.

1. Geriatric + rehab focus
- Emphasize long-standing interest in older adults and memory care.
- Mention Grandma Margaret “Maggie” O’Leary and how much time I spent with her at Providence Manor long-term care home in Kingston, Ontario.
- From September 2019 until she passed away there in February 2022, I visited her on the secured dementia/memory-care unit most weeks. I brought my acoustic guitar and helped with informal sing-alongs for residents and families.
- Those evenings on the locked unit—hearing residents suddenly remember lyrics from old hymns and folk songs—were a huge part of why I chose the Bachelor of Nursing program at the University of Calgary.

2. Other area applying for / specific roles
- On the Carewest form, clearly state: “I’d also be interested in helping with any dementia or memory-care music groups, since I used to play guitar for my grandma Maggie O’Leary’s sing-alongs on the secured unit at Providence Manor from 2019 until she passed away in Feb 2022.”
- Connect this to my current habits: I still bring my guitar to small church gatherings in southeast Calgary and to informal music nights with classmates.

3. Reflection angle
- Tie in my reflective journaling practice (esp. after clinical sims) and how processing grief over Maggie’s death has shaped my empathy with families navigating long-term care decisions.
- Highlight that I’m comfortable in secured units and around residents with moderate to advanced dementia.

To polish later for final application essay.
